skill.name: ux-waiting-audit
skill.description: Audit waiting states in web applications with long-running operations. The skill guides browser-based reviews of loading feedback, progress indicators, screenshots, and recommendations.
skill.summary: Evaluate loading and waiting UX for web apps that take 30 seconds or longer to complete work.
skill.author: BayramAnnakov

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, weak-crypto, hidden-file, URL, and screenshot-upload patterns, but those are Markdown examples, templates, or documentation false positives. The confirmed risk is intended browser automation: the skill opens user-provided URLs, captures screenshots and page text, and can run JavaScript clicks or form submissions. Publish with a warning for authenticated pages and production systems; no malicious intent or prompt injection evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Page State Capture
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs browser audits to take screenshots during navigation and long operations, and it reads visible page text. This is legitimate for UX work, but it can capture customer data, credentials shown on screen, or private product information on authenticated pages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The screenshot and page-text capture instructions are explicit, and the skill also asks about authentication requirements. The risk depends on the target page, so this is a privacy warning rather than evidence of malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Browser JavaScript Can Trigger Page Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends running JavaScript that clicks elements, submits forms, and uses coordinate-based interactions. This supports the audit workflow, but it can trigger real actions if used on production systems without confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly shows JavaScript click and submit examples. The context is a user-directed UX audit, so the risk is operational misuse, not confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts occur in Markdown code fences, checklists, and report templates. I did not find evidence that the skill executes local shell commands or asks the assistant to run arbitrary system commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation examples or browser JavaScript snippets, not executable Ruby or shell code. No installer script or local command runner is shown at those locations.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs And Home Directory Path Are Low Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are project links or an example audit target, and the hidden home directory path is a manual installation location. These are documentation artifacts, not evidence of network exfiltration or hidden persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding README text shows ordinary installation and credit links. There is no code that writes hidden files or sends data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography And Upload Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections point to prose, templates, or unrelated Markdown lines. The screen capture upload alerts point to screenshot timing comments, not upload logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact locations do not contain cryptographic API calls or upload endpoints. Screenshot capture is real, but I did not find evidence of automatic upload behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screenshot And Text Extraction In Browser Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs the assistant to capture screenshots and inspect visible text while auditing target pages. This pattern needs user consent when pages contain sensitive or authenticated data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly call for screenshots and visible text extraction. The pattern is expected for the skill, but it can expose private page content.

dangerous_patterns.title: JavaScript Click And Submit Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes browser JavaScript snippets that click elements and submit forms. This can change application state when used against live systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The action snippets are explicit and easy to verify. The surrounding workflow asks for user guidance, which lowers the maliciousness assessment.

content.user_title: Audit Waiting UX in Web Apps
content.value_statement: Long operations can make users feel stuck or uncertain. This skill reviews waiting states and produces practical UX fixes using screenshots and a structured checklist.
actual_capabilities: Guides browser-based audits of long-running web operations that take 30 seconds or longer.
Captures a timeline of screenshots at start, midpoint, and completion states.
Evaluates loading UX against a 10-point waiting-state checklist.
Checks for progress bars, status text, counters, partial results, and cancel options.
Generates prioritized recommendations, quick wins, and report sections for stakeholders.
limitations: Requires browser automation access and a target web application to inspect.
May need user help to log in, navigate complex flows, or trigger the operation.
Does not replace moderated user research or analytics from real production users.
Screenshots can include sensitive information, so private pages need care.

use_cases.title: Improve AI Task Waiting States
use_cases.description: Audit how an AI product communicates progress during research, generation, or analysis tasks.

use_cases.title: Review SaaS Report Generation
use_cases.description: Find missing feedback, unclear progress, and weak completion moments in slow reporting flows.

use_cases.title: Prepare Launch Quality Checks
use_cases.description: Validate that new workflows show useful waiting feedback before release.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Waiting Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a single known flow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the waiting UX for this URL: [URL]. The operation is [operation]. Ask before triggering any action that changes data.

prompt_templates.title: Checklist-Based Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the waiting-state checklist on [URL]. Capture start, 10-second, 30-second, and completion states. Score each category and list quick wins.

prompt_templates.title: Authenticated Product Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for a logged-in application.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the waiting UX after I log in manually. I will trigger the operation. Capture screenshots only after I confirm the page is ready.

prompt_templates.title: Comparative UX Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the waiting UX for [flow A] and [flow B]. Identify which flow better reduces uncertainty and recommend design changes.

output_examples.input: Audit a report generation flow that takes about 45 seconds.
output: Overall score: 5 of 10 waiting-state categories addressed.
Main gap: the page shows a spinner but no time estimate or progress detail.
Quick win: add status text that names the current processing step.

output_examples.input: Review an AI research task that streams partial results.
output: Strength: partial results appear before completion, which reduces uncertainty.
Risk: there is no stop or use-current-results option.
Recommendation: add a clear early-exit control with preserved partial output.

output_examples.input: Check whether a long import flow supports work while waiting.
output: Finding: the blocking modal prevents navigation during import.
User impact: users feel trapped during a slow background task.
Recommendation: move import progress to a persistent notification area.

best_practices: Use test data or staging environments when the audit requires clicking buttons or submitting forms.
Confirm screenshot permission before auditing authenticated pages or customer records.
Give the skill the exact operation, expected duration, and success state before the audit starts.
anti_patterns: Do not run the audit on sensitive production pages without consent.
Do not ask it to guess destructive actions from screenshots alone.
Do not treat checklist scores as a replacement for user behavior data.

faq.question: What does this skill audit?
faq.answer: It audits loading and waiting states for web operations that take 30 seconds or longer.

faq.question: Does it need browser access?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed for browser automation, screenshots, and simple page-state checks.

faq.question: Can it test authenticated apps?
faq.answer: Yes, but users should log in manually and approve screenshots before capture.

faq.question: Does it run local shell commands?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The detected command patterns are Markdown examples and browser snippets.

faq.question: What output should I expect?
faq.answer: Expect a scored checklist, screenshot timeline, strengths, gaps, quick wins, and prioritized recommendations.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: Use caution. Some suggested browser actions can click buttons or submit forms on the target page.
